attributes.stem:
A 76-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of waking up three to four times per night to pass urine. The frequency of urination during the day is unchanged. He has a medical history of hypertension, ischaemic heart disease, and asthma. His medications include amlodipine, aspirin, atorvastatin, and salbutamol inhaler as needed. He drinks two cups of coffee in the evening and usually goes to bed at 10 PM.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 138/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. There is mild bilateral ankle oedema. Cardiorespiratory examination is otherwise normal. His prostate is not enlarged on digital rectal examination.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with nocturnal polyuria, which is excessive urine production during sleeping hours. In older adults with cardiovascular disease, this is commonly due to redistribution of peripheral oedema when lying flat at night. The presence of ankle oedema and cardiovascular comorbidities supports this mechanism.

Advise evening fluid restriction is the most appropriate first step in management. This includes limiting fluid intake in the hours before bedtime and avoiding evening caffeine consumption, which can act as a diuretic. These simple lifestyle modifications can significantly reduce nocturnal urine production without the need for medications.

Desmopressin can be effective for nocturnal polyuria but carries significant risks in older adults, including hyponatraemia. It should only be considered after simpler interventions have failed and careful patient selection.

Mirabegron, a beta-3 agonist, is used for overactive bladder syndrome. This patient's symptoms are more consistent with nocturnal polyuria rather than bladder storage problems, making this medication inappropriate.

Overnight oximetry would be appropriate if sleep apnoea was suspected. While sleep apnoea can cause nocturia, this patient's presentation is more consistent with fluid redistribution.

Furosemide would be an overly aggressive first step for mild ankle oedema. While diuretics can be helpful for fluid overload, they should be reserved for cases where lifestyle modifications have failed. If prescribed, they should be given in the morning to avoid nocturnal diuresis.

Additional management strategies should include elevating the legs during the day to prevent fluid accumulation and considering compression stockings. If these measures are insufficient, then medication adjustments or the addition of carefully timed diuretics could be considered.
learningPoints: Nocturnal polyuria in older adults is commonly caused by peripheral oedema with nocturnal fluid redistribution.
Timing of diuretic administration can significantly impact nocturnal urine production.
Lifestyle modifications including fluid and caffeine restriction in the evening are important initial management steps.
references: Weiss JP, Everaert K. Management of Nocturia and Nocturnal Polyuria. Urology. 2019 Nov;133S:24-33. doi: 10.1016/j.urology.2019.09.022. Epub 2019 Oct 2. PMID: 31586470. Available online at ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0090429519308507#sec0005
